question_text: On admission of a new partner, the Revaluation Account is prepared to record:
explanation: The Revaluation Account on admission records changes in the values of assets and liabilities that are agreed upon at the time of the new partner's entry. This ensures the new partner buys into the firm at fair/current values, not at outdated book values. The net profit or loss on revaluation is transferred to OLD partners' capital accounts in the OLD ratio.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Changes in the values of assets and liabilities

elimination_hints.hint: New partner's capital is recorded through Cash/Bank and Capital Account entries, not through the Revaluation Account.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing capital introduction with revaluation of existing items.

elimination_hints.hint: Goodwill is adjusted through separate goodwill entries (premium method or raise-and-write-off). Revaluation Account handles tangible assets and liabilities.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up goodwill treatment with revaluation of assets.

elimination_hints.hint: Sacrificing ratio is a calculation (Old − New), not an accounting entry. Revaluation Account records actual value changes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing a ratio computation with an accounting record.

question_text: The net profit or loss on revaluation at the time of admission is transferred to:
explanation: The Revaluation Account balance (profit or loss) is transferred to OLD partners' capital accounts in the OLD profit-sharing ratio. This is because the change in asset/liability values relates to the period before the new partner's admission. The new partner is not entitled to any share of revaluation profit or responsible for any revaluation loss.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Old partners' capital accounts in old ratio
All partners' capital accounts in new ratio

elimination_hints.hint: New ratio includes the new partner. The new partner didn't exist when asset values changed, so they shouldn't share in revaluation gains/losses.
elimination_hints.misconception: Including the new partner in revaluation distribution.

elimination_hints.hint: The new partner was not part of the firm when values changed. Only old partners bear the revaluation profit/loss.
elimination_hints.misconception: Attributing prior-period adjustments to the incoming partner.

elimination_hints.hint: General Reserve is a separate accumulated profit. Revaluation balance goes directly to old partners' capitals, not to another reserve.
elimination_hints.misconception: Routing revaluation through a reserve account instead of directly to partners.

question_text: A and B share profits in 3:2. C is admitted. On revaluation: Building increased by ₹1,00,000, Machinery decreased by ₹40,000, Stock decreased by ₹10,000, Creditors understated by ₹20,000, and an unrecorded investment of ₹30,000 was found. Net revaluation result is:
explanation:
Revaluation Account:
Credits (gains): Building +₹1,00,000, Unrecorded investment +₹30,000 = ₹1,30,000.
Debits (losses): Machinery −₹40,000, Stock −₹10,000, Creditors understated (increase) ₹20,000 = ₹70,000.
Net = ₹1,30,000 − ₹70,000 = ₹60,000 Profit.
Note: Understated creditors means actual creditors are MORE than books — increasing liability is a loss (debit).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹80,000 might result from not recording the understated creditors (₹20,000 loss). Understated creditors = liability increased = loss to firm.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring the creditors adjustment or treating it as a gain.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹50,000 might result from not including the unrecorded investment (₹30,000 gain). Unrecorded asset discovery = gain (credit).
elimination_hints.misconception: Missing the unrecorded investment from the gains.

elimination_hints.hint: Credits (₹1,30,000) exceed debits (₹70,000), so it's a profit, not a loss.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the gains and losses in the Revaluation Account.

question_text: On admission, the existing General Reserve of ₹60,000 is distributed among old partners A and B (sharing 2:1). The journal entry is:
explanation: General Reserve represents accumulated undistributed profits. On admission, it must be transferred to old partners in their old ratio before the new partner enters. Entry: Dr. General Reserve A/c ₹60,000, Cr. A's Capital A/c ₹40,000 (60,000 × 2/3), Cr. B's Capital A/c ₹20,000 (60,000 × 1/3).
subject: accountancy

options.text: Dr. A ₹40,000, Dr. B ₹20,000; Cr. General Reserve ₹60,000
Dr. General Reserve ₹60,000; Cr. A ₹30,000, Cr. B ₹30,000
Dr. General Reserve ₹60,000; Cr. A ₹20,000, Cr. B ₹40,000
Dr. General Reserve ₹60,000; Cr. A ₹40,000, Cr. B ₹20,000

elimination_hints.hint: This reverses the entry — debiting partners and crediting reserve. Distributing a reserve means DEBITING the reserve (closing it) and CREDITING partners.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the debit and credit sides of the distribution entry.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹30,000 each assumes equal sharing. A and B share 2:1, so A = 40,000 and B = 20,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Distributing equally instead of in the agreed profit-sharing ratio.

elimination_hints.hint: This gives A ₹20,000 and B ₹40,000 — the ratio 1:2, which is reversed. A (2 share) gets ₹40,000 and B (1 share) gets ₹20,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping A's and B's shares — giving the smaller share to the larger partner.

question_text: P and Q are partners sharing in 3:2. R is admitted for 1/5th share. R brings ₹2,00,000 as capital. Total capital of the new firm should be proportionate to profit-sharing ratio. Combined capital of P and Q after all adjustments is ₹5,60,000. R's proportionate capital should be:
explanation:
R's share = 1/5. Old partners' combined share = 4/5.
If old partners' combined capital = ₹5,60,000 for 4/5 share, then total proportionate capital = 5,60,000 × 5/4 = ₹7,00,000.
R's proportionate capital for 1/5 share = 7,00,000 × 1/5 = ₹1,40,000.
R brings ₹2,00,000. Excess = 2,00,000 − 1,40,000 = ₹60,000. This excess could be treated as goodwill (hidden goodwill).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹2,00,000 is what R actually brings, not the proportionate amount. Proportionate capital = total capital × R's share = 7,00,000 × 1/5 = ₹1,40,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing actual capital brought with proportionate capital required.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹1,00,000 doesn't match any calculation. Total capital = 5,60,000 / (4/5) = 7,00,000. R's share = 7,00,000 × 1/5 = ₹1,40,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Arithmetic error or using wrong formula.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹1,60,000 = 5,60,000/3.5. This uses an incorrect divisor. Correct: 5,60,000 represents 4/5 share, so full capital = 5,60,000 × 5/4.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using an incorrect multiplier to find total capital.

question_text: A and B share profits in 3:2. C is admitted for 1/4th share. C brings ₹3,00,000 as capital. The total capital of the new firm based on C's capital is:
subtopic: Total capital based on new partner's contribution
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹9,00,000 = 3,00,000 × 3. This treats C's share as 1/3 instead of 1/4. C gets 1/4, so total = 3,00,000 × 4.
elimination_hints.misconception: Multiplying by 3 instead of 4 — confusing 1/4 share with 1/3.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹15,00,000 = 3,00,000 × 5. This treats C's share as 1/5 instead of 1/4.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the wrong fraction for C's share.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹6,00,000 = 3,00,000 × 2. This treats C's share as 1/2. C's actual share is 1/4, so total = 3,00,000 × 4 = ₹12,00,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using an incorrect multiplier.

question_text: Hidden goodwill on admission is calculated when:
explanation: Hidden goodwill arises when the new partner's capital contribution implies a total firm value higher than the combined book value of the firm's net assets. It is calculated when the new partner brings capital that, when capitalised at their share percentage, exceeds the total adjusted capital of the firm. Formula: Hidden Goodwill = Total capitalised value (based on new partner's capital) − Net assets of the firm (after revaluation).
subject: accountancy

options.text: Goodwill is already recorded in the books
New partner's capital implies a firm value higher than net assets
Old partners refuse to value goodwill
New partner brings excess cash only

elimination_hints.hint: If goodwill is already in books, it's visible, not hidden. Hidden goodwill is INFERRED from the capital contribution, not shown in books.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing existing goodwill with hidden (implied) goodwill.

elimination_hints.hint: Hidden goodwill is calculated based on the new partner's capital contribution, not based on old partners' willingness. It's an arithmetic inference.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking hidden goodwill is a subjective decision rather than a computed value.

elimination_hints.hint: Excess cash alone doesn't define hidden goodwill. The excess must be computed by comparing total capitalised value with net assets.
elimination_hints.misconception: Equating any excess cash with hidden goodwill without proper computation.

question_text: L and M share profits in 2:1. N is admitted for 1/4th share. Balance Sheet shows: Debtors ₹80,000, Provision for Doubtful Debts ₹4,000. It is agreed to maintain provision at 10% of debtors. The Revaluation Account will be:
explanation:
Current provision = ₹4,000. Required provision = 10% of 80,000 = ₹8,000.
Increase in provision = 8,000 − 4,000 = ₹4,000.
Increasing a provision is a loss — Revaluation Account is DEBITED by ₹4,000.
Entry: Dr. Revaluation A/c ₹4,000, Cr. Provision for Doubtful Debts A/c ₹4,000.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Credit = gain. Increasing provision means expecting more bad debts = LOSS. It should be debited.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating an increase in provision as a gain.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹8,000 is the total required provision. Only the INCREASE (8,000 − 4,000 = 4,000) goes to Revaluation Account.
elimination_hints.misconception: Recording the full new provision instead of just the change.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹8,000 is the total provision needed, but the existing provision is ₹4,000. Only the increase of ₹4,000 is debited.
elimination_hints.misconception: Debiting the full amount instead of the incremental change.

question_text: On admission, if a firm has a P&L Account showing a debit balance of ₹30,000, it is:
explanation: A debit balance on P&L Account means accumulated losses. On admission, this must be distributed (debited) to old partners in their old ratio — the new partner should not bear pre-existing losses. Entry: Dr. Old Partners' Capital A/cs (old ratio) ₹30,000, Cr. P&L A/c ₹30,000.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Debited to old partners' capitals in old ratio
Debited to all partners in new ratio
Debited to new partner's capital only

elimination_hints.hint: Revaluation Account handles changes in ASSET and LIABILITY values, not the distribution of accumulated P&L balances.
elimination_hints.misconception: Routing P&L balance through Revaluation Account.

elimination_hints.hint: New ratio includes the new partner. The new partner shouldn't bear losses that occurred before their admission.
elimination_hints.misconception: Burdening the new partner with pre-admission losses.

elimination_hints.hint: The new partner has no responsibility for old losses. Only old partners bear pre-existing losses in their old ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Making the new partner solely responsible for old losses.

question_text: X and Y share profits in 3:2. Z is admitted for 1/5th share bringing ₹4,00,000 as capital. After all adjustments, X's adjusted capital is ₹5,00,000 and Y's is ₹3,00,000. Total capital of new firm should be proportionate. Calculate adjustments needed for X and Y.
explanation:
Z brings ₹4,00,000 for 1/5th share. Total capital = 4,00,000 × 5 = ₹20,00,000.
New ratio: X's share (sacrifice in old ratio) = 4/5 × 3/5 = 12/25. Y's share = 4/5 × 2/5 = 8/25. Z = 1/5 = 5/25.
X's required capital = 20,00,000 × 12/25 = ₹9,60,000. X's current = ₹5,00,000. X brings ₹4,60,000.
Y's required capital = 20,00,000 × 8/25 = ₹6,40,000. Y's current = ₹3,00,000. Y brings ₹3,40,000.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹5,00,000 and ₹3,00,000 are their CURRENT capitals. Required capitals are ₹9,60,000 and ₹6,40,000 respectively.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubling current capitals instead of computing proportionate amounts.
Using incorrect proportionate capital calculations.

elimination_hints.hint: Both X and Y have LESS than required. Neither should withdraw. X needs ₹4,60,000 more and Y needs ₹3,40,000 more.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking one partner has excess capital when both fall short.

question_text: When a Workmen Compensation Reserve of ₹50,000 exists and there is a claim of ₹20,000 at the time of admission, the treatment is:
explanation:
WCR = ₹50,000. Claim = ₹20,000.
Step 1: Use ₹20,000 from the reserve for the claim. Entry: Dr. WCR ₹20,000, Cr. Workmen Compensation Claim (liability) ₹20,000.
Step 2: Remaining ₹30,000 is distributed to old partners in old ratio. Entry: Dr. WCR ₹30,000, Cr. Old Partners' Capitals (old ratio) ₹30,000.
The new partner doesn't share in the reserve as it was accumulated before admission.
subject: accountancy

options.text: ₹20,000 for claim; ₹30,000 to old partners in old ratio
₹50,000 entirely to old partners in old ratio
₹20,000 for claim; ₹30,000 to all partners in new ratio

elimination_hints.hint: There is an actual claim of ₹20,000. This must be paid from the reserve first. Only the balance (₹30,000) is distributable.
elimination_hints.misconception: Distributing the entire reserve without settling the actual claim.

elimination_hints.hint: The balance ₹30,000 goes to OLD partners in OLD ratio. The new partner has no claim on pre-existing reserves.
elimination_hints.misconception: Including the new partner in reserve distribution.

elimination_hints.hint: The claim is only ₹20,000. Using ₹50,000 for a ₹20,000 claim wastes ₹30,000 that rightfully belongs to old partners.
elimination_hints.misconception: Over-applying the reserve beyond the actual claim amount.

question_text: On admission, an unrecorded liability of ₹15,000 is discovered. In the Revaluation Account, it is:
explanation: An unrecorded liability means the firm owes more than the books show. Recording it increases liabilities, which is a LOSS. In the Revaluation Account: Dr. Revaluation A/c ₹15,000, Cr. Liability A/c ₹15,000. The loss is ultimately borne by old partners in old ratio.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Credited to the new partner's capital

elimination_hints.hint: Credit = gain. Discovering an unrecorded liability is a LOSS (more to pay than expected). It is debited.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating a new liability as a gain.

elimination_hints.hint: All changes in assets and liabilities during reconstitution must be recorded through the Revaluation Account.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking unrecorded items can be ignored.

elimination_hints.hint: The liability existed before the new partner joined. It's a loss for OLD partners, recorded through Revaluation Account and shared in old ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Charging pre-existing liabilities to the new partner.

question_text:
Assertion (A): On admission of a new partner, the old partners' capital accounts may need to be adjusted to make them proportionate to the new profit-sharing ratio.
Reason (R): If the total capital of the new firm is decided based on the new partner's contribution, old partners must bring in or withdraw cash to align their capitals with their share in the new ratio.
Select the correct option:
explanation: The Assertion is TRUE — capital adjustment ensures each partner's capital is proportionate to their share. The Reason is also TRUE — when total capital is determined from the new partner's contribution, old partners adjust by bringing in additional capital (if short) or withdrawing excess (if over). R correctly explains A — the need for proportionality drives the adjustment.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains the mechanism of capital adjustment — proportionality based on new partner's capital. Clear causal link.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not seeing the connection between proportionality requirement and the adjustment mechanism.

elimination_hints.hint: R IS true — partners do bring in or withdraw to align capitals proportionately.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the capital adjustment mechanism.

elimination_hints.hint: A IS true — capital adjustment is a standard procedure on admission when proportionate capitals are required.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing that capital adjustment is a common admission procedure.

question_text:
Assertion (A): When the new partner brings capital in excess of their proportionate share, the excess may represent hidden goodwill.
Reason (R): The excess capital implies the firm's true value (including goodwill) is higher than its book value.
Select the correct option:
explanation: The Assertion is TRUE — when the new partner pays more than the proportionate book value, the excess represents hidden/implied goodwill. For example, if 1/4 share's book value is ₹1,00,000 but the new partner brings ₹1,50,000, the ₹50,000 excess implies goodwill. The Reason is also TRUE — the excess payment implies the partner values the firm above its book value, the difference being goodwill. R correctly explains A.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains WHY excess capital = hidden goodwill — because the firm's true value exceeds book value. Direct causal link.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not connecting the valuation gap with goodwill.

elimination_hints.hint: R IS true — the new partner pays excess precisely because they perceive the firm's value (including intangibles) as higher than book value.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the economic logic behind hidden goodwill.

elimination_hints.hint: A IS true — this is a standard accounting concept. Excess capital from the new partner is a classic indicator of hidden goodwill.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognizing hidden goodwill as a valid concept.

question_text: On admission of a new partner, the new partner shares in the revaluation profit along with old partners.
explanation: FALSE. Revaluation profit (or loss) is shared ONLY among old partners in the old ratio. The new partner was not part of the firm when asset values changed, so they have no right to revaluation gains (or responsibility for losses). Revaluation adjusts values to current levels BEFORE the new partner joins.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: The new partner joined AFTER the values changed. Only old partners share revaluation profit/loss in old ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Including the new partner in pre-admission value changes.

question_text: If no goodwill amount is mentioned in the question and the new partner brings excess capital over proportionate share, the excess may be treated as premium for goodwill.
explanation: TRUE. When the new partner's capital exceeds the proportionate amount and no separate goodwill is specified, the excess is treated as hidden/implied goodwill (premium). This excess is credited to old partners in the sacrificing ratio as compensation for the goodwill of the firm that they built.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: When a new partner pays more than book value proportion, they are implicitly paying for the firm's goodwill. This excess is a valid form of premium.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking excess capital must always be returned as cash to the new partner.

question_text:
Match the Balance Sheet item with its treatment on admission of a new partner:

Column I:
(i) General Reserve
(ii) Revaluation Loss
(iii) P&L Account (Credit balance)
(iv) Advertisement Suspense Account

Column II:
(P) Debited to old partners in old ratio (write off)
(Q) Credited to old partners in old ratio (distributed)
(R) Debited to old partners in old ratio (loss shared)
(S) Credited to old partners in old ratio (profit distributed)
explanation: (i) General Reserve → (Q): Accumulated profit credited to old partners in old ratio. (ii) Revaluation Loss → (R): Loss debited to old partners in old ratio. (iii) P&L Credit balance → (S): Undistributed profit credited to old partners in old ratio. (iv) Advertisement Suspense → (P): Fictitious asset written off — debited to old partners in old ratio.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: This matches Revaluation Loss (ii) with (P) write-off. But (P) is for fictitious assets. Revaluation loss goes to (R) — loss shared in old ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing revaluation loss treatment with fictitious asset write-off.

elimination_hints.hint: This swaps General Reserve (i) with P&L Credit (iii). Both are credits to old partners, but Q specifically describes 'distributed' (reserve) and S describes 'profit distributed' (P&L balance).
elimination_hints.misconception: Not distinguishing between the specific descriptions of Q and S.

elimination_hints.hint: This reverses nearly everything — treating reserves as losses and losses as profits.
elimination_hints.misconception: Fundamentally confusing credits and debits in admission adjustments.

question_text: On admission, when the new partner's capital is more than the proportionate amount and the excess cannot be attributed to goodwill, the excess is transferred to the new partner's _______ Account.
explanation: When the new partner brings more capital than their proportionate share and it is not treated as goodwill premium, the excess is transferred to the new partner's Current Account (as a credit balance). This means the new partner can withdraw this excess later. Alternatively, if the partners agree, the excess can be returned in cash. Under the fixed capital method, excess goes to Current Account. Under fluctuating method, it stays in Capital Account but may be noted separately.
subtopic: Excess capital not attributed to goodwill
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: A Loan Account would imply the firm borrowed from the partner. Excess capital is not a loan — it's part of the partner's investment.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating excess capital as a loan from the partner.

elimination_hints.hint: General Reserve is an accumulated firm reserve, not a personal partner account. Excess goes to the partner's own Current Account.
elimination_hints.misconception: Routing individual partner excess into a firm reserve.

elimination_hints.hint: Revaluation Account handles asset/liability value changes. Excess capital has nothing to do with revaluation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using Revaluation Account for capital surplus.

question_text: G and H share profits in 3:2. I is admitted for 1/5th share, contributing ₹2,00,000 as capital. Balance Sheet before admission: Assets ₹8,00,000, Liabilities ₹2,00,000, G's Capital ₹3,60,000, H's Capital ₹2,40,000. No goodwill or reserves exist. On revaluation, assets increase by ₹40,000 and liabilities decrease by ₹10,000. Is there hidden goodwill? If yes, how much?
explanation:
Step 1: Revaluation profit = Asset increase 40,000 + Liability decrease 10,000 = ₹50,000.
G's share = 50,000 × 3/5 = ₹30,000. H's share = 50,000 × 2/5 = ₹20,000.
Step 2: Adjusted capitals: G = 3,60,000 + 30,000 = ₹3,90,000. H = 2,40,000 + 20,000 = ₹2,60,000.
Step 3: Total adjusted old capital = 3,90,000 + 2,60,000 = ₹6,50,000 (for 4/5 share).
Step 4: I brings ₹2,00,000 for 1/5. Total implied = 2,00,000 × 5 = ₹10,00,000.
Step 5: Old partners' capital for 4/5 share should be = 10,00,000 × 4/5 = ₹8,00,000.
Step 6: Adjusted old capital = ₹6,50,000. Shortfall = 8,00,000 − 6,50,000 = ₹1,50,000.
This shortfall = Hidden Goodwill = ₹1,50,000.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: I pays ₹2,00,000 for 1/5 share, implying total firm value = ₹10,00,000. But adjusted net assets = only ₹8,50,000. The gap (₹1,50,000) is hidden goodwill.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not computing the implied firm value from the new partner's capital.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹2,00,000 is I's total capital, not the goodwill. Hidden goodwill = Implied value (10,00,000) − Adjusted net assets (6,50,000 + 2,00,000 = 8,50,000) = ₹1,50,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the new partner's entire capital as goodwill.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹50,000 is the revaluation profit, not goodwill. Hidden goodwill = 10,00,000 − 8,50,000 = ₹1,50,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing revaluation profit with hidden goodwill.

question_text:
Arrange the adjustments on admission in the order they affect the old partners' capital accounts:
(i) Goodwill premium distributed
(ii) Accumulated reserves distributed
(iii) Capital brought in by new partner recorded
(iv) Revaluation profit/loss transferred
(v) Existing goodwill written off
explanation: The adjustments affect old partners' capitals in this order: (1) Write off existing goodwill in old ratio (v) — debit to old partners. (2) Distribute accumulated reserves in old ratio (ii) — credit to old partners. (3) Transfer revaluation profit/loss in old ratio (iv) — credit (profit) or debit (loss). (4) Distribute goodwill premium in sacrificing ratio (i) — credit to old partners. (5) Record new partner's capital (iii) — doesn't directly affect old partners' capitals. Order: (v) → (ii) → (iv) → (i) → (iii).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Recording new partner's capital (iii) should be the LAST step. All old-period adjustments must be completed before the new partner's entry.
elimination_hints.misconception: Bringing in the new partner before completing all adjustments.

elimination_hints.hint: Distributing goodwill premium (i) before writing off existing goodwill (v) creates confusion. Old goodwill must be cleared first.
elimination_hints.misconception: Making new goodwill adjustments before clearing old goodwill.

elimination_hints.hint: Recording new capital (iii) before goodwill distribution (i) and goodwill write-off (v) is out of order. All adjustments precede the new partner's entry.
elimination_hints.misconception: Interleaving new partner entry with old-period adjustments.
